content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Inte
(916) 322-5642          322-5660          322-5901          322-5441          322-5444

May 18, 1982


Mr. Dick Swayne
4427 Freeport Blvd.
Sacramento, CA  95822


Dear Mr. Swayne:

     This letter will confirm my advice to you during our recent
telephone conversation in which you asked about the requirements
for reporting volunteer professional services being donated to a
campaign, specifically an employee or partner of a public relations
firm donating volunteer services to a campaign.

     Government Code Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act
which defines the term contribution states in part:

     Notwithstanding the foregoing definition of "contribution,"
the term does not include volunteer personal services or
payments made by any individual for his or her own travel
expenses if such payments are made voluntarily without any
understanding or agreement that they shall be, directly or
indirectly, repaid to him or her.

     If the employee or partner of a public relations firm does
not receive compensation from the campaign and is providing the
services on his or her own time (time not compensated by the firm)
such volunteer activity is not reportable as stated above.  However,
if any of the public relations firm's supplies or materials are
used, or other employees, such as a secretary, are providing
services for 10% or more of their compensated time, then such goods
or services are reportable as non-monetary contributions from the
public relations firm to the campaign.

     Please feel free to contact me if I can be of any further
assistance.

                                        Yours Very Truly,


                                        [signature]
                                        Lynn Montgomery
                                        Political Reform Consultant

LM:st

sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting requirements for volunteer professional services donated to a campaign, specifically when an employee or partner of a public relations firm provides volunteer services?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Volunteer personal services provided by a public relations firm employee or partner are not reportable if unpaid and performed on the volunteer's own time. However, if the firm's supplies, materials, or other employees (providing 10% or more of compensated time) are used, these must be reported as non-monetary contributions from the firm.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with greeting and signature. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but content structure follows typical FPPC response pattern. Letter references prior telephone conversation. Statute citation (Gov. Code §82015) provided to support advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting requirements for volunteer professional services donated to a campaign, specifically when an employee or partner of a public relations firm provides volunteer services?

CONCLUSION: Volunteer personal services provided by a public relations firm employee or partner are not reportable if unpaid and performed on the volunteer's own time. However, if the firm's supplies, materials, or other employees (providing 10% or more of compensated time) are used, these must be reported as non-monetary contributions from the firm.

Government Code Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act which defines the term contribution states in part: Notwithstanding the foregoing definition of "contribution," the term does not include volunteer personal services or payments made by any individual for his or her own travel expenses if such payments are made voluntarily without any understanding or agreement that they shall be, directly or indirectly, repaid to him or her. If the employee or partner of a public relations firm does not receive compensation from the campaign and is providing the services on his or her own time (time not compensated by the firm) such volunteer activity is not reportable as stated above. However, if any of the public relations firm's supplies or materials are used, or other employees, such as a secretary, are providing services for 10% or more of their compensated time, then such goods or services are reportable as non-monetary contributions from the public relations firm to the campaign. Please feel free to contact me if I can be of any further assistance. Yours Very Truly, [signature] Lynn Montgomery Political Reform Consultant LM:st
embedding.summary: FPPC confirms that volunteer professional services from a public relations firm employee are not reportable contributions if unpaid and performed on personal time, but becomes reportable if firm resources or significant employee time are used.
